parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Craft pickle RCE payload 2) base64-encode 3) set state cookie 4) GET /resume => shell

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Mass assignment -> privilege escalation -> access all protected endpoints
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation: any authenticated user can grant themselves admin or support role.
parsed_findings.proof: PATCH /api/users/123 with body {"isAdmin":true} directly merges into user object before save.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send PATCH /api/users/:id with JSON body containing privileged fields like isAdmin or isSupport set to true.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass assignment via Object.assign allows attacker to overwrite any user field including role/isAdmin from req.body.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Broken auth check -> unauthorized user update -> combined with mass assignment for full privilege escalation
parsed_findings.impact: Broken access control: any authenticated user can modify any other user's data.
parsed_findings.proof: req.user.id req.user.isSupport is a syntax error or no-op; correct form needs !== targetId || !req.user.isSupport logic.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Any authenticated user can PATCH any other user's record since the guard condition never properly evaluates.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authorization check uses bitwise/missing operator instead of logical OR, making the condition always falsy and bypassing access control.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: IDOR -> arbitrary user record write -> chain with mass assignment for privilege escalation
parsed_findings.impact: Any authenticated user can overwrite another user's profile data.
parsed_findings.proof: findById(req.params.id) uses attacker-controlled URL param with no check that it matches the authenticated user's id.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticated as user 1, send PATCH /api/users/2 to modify user 2's record.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No ownership validation ensures req.params.id belongs to req.user, enabling IDOR to modify any user record.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Auth as user with balance 100. 2) Fire 2 concurrent POST /transfer {to:attacker,amount:100}. 3) Observe balance=-100, attacker+200.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker drains account below zero, crediting recipient(s) with more than the sender held.
parsed_findings.proof: Two simultaneous POST /transfer requests both read src before either write commits, allowing balance to go negative.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two concurrent requests with amount=full_balance; both pass the src<amt check before either db.set_balance executes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Race condition (TOCTOU) allows double-spend via concurrent transfer requests

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) POST /transfer {to:victim_id, amount:-100}. 2) Sender balance += 100, victim balance -= 100.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can steal funds from any account they can name by sending a negative transfer to that account.
parsed_findings.proof: int(request.json['amount']) with amt=-100 passes src<amt check (src >= -100), then subtracts a negative (adds) to sender.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer {to:victim, amount:-100}; sender's balance increases, victim's decreases with no authorization from victim.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: No validation that amount is positive allows negative transfers to reverse fund flow

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Enumerate or guess privileged user IDs. 2) POST /transfer {to:admin_id, amount:X}. 3) Funds credit attacker-controlled or privileged account.
parsed_findings.impact: Fund loss to sender with no recourse; potential privilege escalation if a reserved internal ID is targeted.
parsed_findings.proof: db.get_balance(to) is called without verifying to is a valid/existing user, enabling fund injection into arbitrary IDs.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer {to:nonexistent_id, amount:1}; funds leave sender but may be deposited into a phantom or admin account.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Recipient account 'to' is fully attacker-controlled with no ownership or existence validation

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge JWT with admin role -> send request -> verify throws -> decode returns forged payload -> privilege escalation
parsed_findings.impact: Full authentication bypass — attacker can forge arbitrary claims (sub, role, etc.) and be treated as any user.
parsed_findings.proof: If jwt.verify throws (e.g. expired, wrong key, malformed), the catch block calls jwt.decode which performs NO signature verification, returning attacker-controlled claims as trusted.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a Bearer token with a valid JWT structure but invalid/forged signature; in production NODE_ENV the handler returns decoded payload as authenticated.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: JWT signature verification bypassed in production on any verify failure

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit timing oracle to forge valid HMAC -> send crafted payload -> arbitrary process_event execution

parsed_findings.proof: Python `str ==` is not constant-time; attacker can brute-force HMAC byte-by-byte by measuring response latency.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Timing side-channel: string equality `==` leaks signature length/content via variable-time comparison.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger NULL deref -> crash -> if kernel doesn't map page 0, DoS; on some embedded targets page 0 is mapped enabling write-what-where.
parsed_findings.impact: Null-pointer dereference on every legitimate call path; denial of service or potential code execution depending on platform memory layout.
parsed_findings.proof: A non-null `out` pointer always satisfies `out || out_sz==0` as true, so the function returns -1 for every valid call; a NULL `out` with nonzero `out_sz` evaluates false and proceeds to dereference NULL at memcpy.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call copy_name("hello", NULL, 10); — guard is false, memcpy writes to NULL, causing NULL-ptr deref / crash.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Guard condition is inverted: `if (out || out_sz == 0)` should be `if (!out || out_sz == 0)`, so null `out` passes and valid pointers are rejected.
